stories.title: Create your identity
stories.intro: Register a WebAuthn passkey — no password, ever. Your OpenApe ID becomes the DDISA authority for your identity: every Service Provider logs you in via DNS-discovered SSO without ever seeing a credential.

steps.title: Open OpenApe ID
steps.caption: The landing page asks for nothing but your email. Click **Create account** to start.

steps.title: Request your registration link
steps.caption: Enter your email — OpenApe ID sends you a one-time registration link.

steps.title: Register your passkey
steps.caption: The link opens the passkey ceremony. Your device (Touch ID, Windows Hello, a security key) creates the credential — in this E2E run a virtual authenticator answers headlessly.

steps.title: Done — you are signed in
steps.caption: That's the whole sign-up: one passkey, no password to remember or leak.

stories.title: Your account dashboard
stories.intro: Your dashboard is home base for your identity. Passkeys, SSH keys, agents, permissions, delegations and connected services each have their own page, so you go straight to the one you need.

steps.title: Open your dashboard
steps.caption: Once you're signed in, the dashboard brings together everything tied to your identity — passkeys, SSH keys, agents, permissions, delegations and connected services. Choose an area to open it.

steps.title: Manage your passkeys
steps.caption: Open **Passkeys** to manage how you sign in — add a new device, or remove one you no longer use.

steps.title: Manage your SSH keys
steps.caption: **SSH keys** holds the public keys for *Sign in with SSH Key*. Paste a key to add it, or remove one you've retired.

steps.title: Review connected services
steps.caption: **Connected services** lists the apps you've signed in to with your OpenApe ID. Revoke one and you'll be asked to approve it again next time. Apps acting for you at *another* service live under **Delegations**.

stories.title: Recover your account — or stop an attack in one tap
stories.intro: Lost every device with a passkey? Account recovery lets you enrol a new one after a waiting period — and because every recovery attempt is announced loudly on all your channels, an attacker can never run one quietly. This is the whole flow, from both sides.

steps.title: Start a recovery
steps.caption: On the sign-in page choose **Lost access?** — no password reset, no support ticket. Recovery only ever grants permission to register a new passkey; it never signs anyone in by itself.

steps.title: Request it for your email
steps.caption: Enter your account email. OpenApe ID answers the same way whether the address has an account or not, so nobody can use this form to probe for accounts. If it is yours, the waiting period starts now.

steps.title: Every channel gets the warning
steps.caption: The warning goes out immediately: a push notification to every device you enabled notifications on, and a mail to every address that was ever linked to your account — even ones you replaced years ago. A single compromised mailbox cannot swallow the alarm. Each warning names the exact moment the recovery could complete and carries a one-tap **Cancel recovery** link that works without signing in.

steps.title: Cancel it in one tap
steps.caption: Didn't request it? Tap **Cancel recovery** in any warning — or right here in your account — and the attempt is dead for good. A cancelled recovery can never be completed, not even after its waiting period would have ended. Signing in with one of your existing passkeys cancels it automatically, too.

stories.title: Vacation mode: a longer shield while you are away
stories.intro: The recovery waiting period adapts to how you use your account: 7 days while you are active, 72 hours once an account has been dormant for a month. Going off-grid? Vacation mode stretches the shield to up to 14 days so nobody can take over your account while you cannot react.

steps.title: Open your account settings
steps.caption: Vacation mode lives in your account settings. Only you, signed in, can change it — there is no way to flip it from the outside.

steps.title: Switch on vacation mode
steps.caption: Switch it on and pick how long a recovery attempt has to wait — up to 14 days, which is also the hard maximum. While it is on, the vacation wait applies no matter how recently you signed in.

steps.title: Your shield is set
steps.caption: That's it. A recovery requested from now on is bound to the wait that applied at the moment of the request — switching vacation mode off later never shortens a deadline that is already running.

stories.title: See every recovery attempt — nothing disappears
stories.intro: Every recovery attempt against your account is on permanent record: when it happened, where it came from, and how it ended. Attackers cannot probe quietly, and nobody — not even you — can scrub the record.

steps.title: Open your account settings
steps.caption: The recovery history sits in your account settings, visible only to you while signed in.

steps.title: Review every attempt
steps.caption: Each entry shows when the attempt was made, where it came from (IP address and browser, as far as known) and what became of it: still running — including the moment it could complete — finished, cancelled, or expired unused. Entries contain no links or codes an attacker could reuse, and they can neither be edited nor deleted: the cancelled attempt from the previous chapter stays on record forever.

stories.title: Your agent identities
stories.intro: Agents are first-class identities under your account: the IdP mints them a DDISA email and you stay in control.

steps.title: Open Agents at your IdP
steps.caption: Every agent spawned from your nests appears here with its own `…@id.openape.ai` identity — inspect it, or revoke it independently of your own keys.
